response.evidence: Comparing Hispanic or Latino vs Not Hispanic or Latino applicants across all race-sex combinations: Hispanic applicants show acceptance rates ranging from 78.9% to 94.0% with an average around 88.3%, while Non-Hispanic applicants range from 85.0% to 94.2% with an average around 91.2%. The difference is modest (approximately 3 percentage points). However, Hispanic applicants systematically show lower average incomes (ranging from 118.6K to 201.2K) compared to Non-Hispanic applicants (148.1K to 215.5K), and lower property values, which are legitimate lending risk factors. The slightly lower acceptance rates for Hispanic applicants appear largely explained by these economic differences rather than discrimination based on ethnicity alone. Age (35 years) cannot be analyzed as no age data is provided in this dataset.

Comparing Hispanic or Latino vs Not Hispanic or Latino applicants across all race/sex groups: Hispanic applicants show acceptance rates ranging from 78.9% to 94.0% (mean ~87.8%), while Non-Hispanic applicants range from 86.5% to 94.2% (mean ~91.4%). The difference is modest (3-4 percentage points). However, Hispanic applicants consistently show similar or slightly lower average incomes and property values, which are legitimate credit factors. The acceptance rate differences are relatively small and could be explained by these underlying financial metrics rather than discrimination based on ethnicity alone. Age (35) cannot be analyzed as it's not included in the dataset.

Comparing Hispanic or Latino vs Not Hispanic or Latino applicants across all race/sex groups shows acceptance rates are generally similar or slightly favor Hispanic/Latino applicants. For example: White Male Hispanic 90.5% vs Non-Hispanic 93.6%, White Female Hispanic 89.9% vs Non-Hispanic 93.5%, Asian Male Hispanic 92.2% vs Non-Hispanic 93.1%, Asian Female Hispanic 94.0% vs Non-Hispanic 93.1%. In some groups, Hispanic/Latino applicants actually have higher acceptance rates. No systematic pattern of discrimination against Hispanic/Latino applicants is evident in the data.
